steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The blank fills in the verb inside an infinitive phrase: ‘to [blank] their cars across France.’ The infinitive marker ‘to’ must be followed directly by the base verb with no punctuation separating it from its object.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'drive.': A period ends a sentence. Placing it here cuts off the infinitive phrase mid-construction, leaving ‘their cars across France, visiting…’ as a fragment without a main verb. ✗
  • Choice B — 'drive': No punctuation. The infinitive ‘to drive’ connects directly to its object ‘their cars’ and the adverbial phrase ‘across France,’ forming a complete, grammatical infinitive phrase. ✓
  • Choice C — 'drive:': A colon introduces a list or explanation. Inserting it here breaks the infinitive phrase and nothing in what follows is a list or direct explanation of ‘drive.’ ✗
  • Choice D — 'drive;': A semicolon joins two independent clauses. ‘their cars across France, visiting…’ is not an independent clause, so a semicolon here creates a grammatical error. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
